LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANT TIJUANA'S PRODUCE, INC.'S MOTION SEEKING TO CHANGE VENUE. A R G U E D. THE COURT ADOPTS THE TENTATIVE RULING. DENIED. ON A MOTION TO CHANGE VENUE, THE BURDEN IS ON THE MOVING PARTY TO SHOW (1) THAT VENUE IS PROPER IN A DIFFERENT COUNTY THAN THE ONE IN WHICH THE LAWSUIT WAS FILED AND (2) THAT VENUE IS IMPROPER IN THE CURRENT COUNTY UNDER ANY APPLICABLE THEORY. (LA MIRADA COMMUNITY HOSPITAL V. SUPERIOR COURT (1967) 249 CAL.APP.2D 39, 42.) ALTHOUGH DEFENDANT HAS ESTABLISHED THAT LOS ANGELES COUNTY WOULD BE A PROPER VENUE, IT HAS FAILED TO SHOW THAT VENUE IS IMPROPER IN SAN FRANCISCO COUNTY UNDER ANY APPLICABLE THEORY. (SEE WORD MINUTES FOR FULL DETAILS) JUDGE: ETHAN P. SCHULMAN, CLERK: M. GOODMAN, COURT REPORTER: ANTHONY VAUGHN, CSR#6185, TONYVAUGHN@YAHOO.COM

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT TIJUANA'S PRODUCE, INC.'s MOTION FOR ATTORNEY FEES AND EXPENSES.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Tijuana Produce, Inc.'s (TPI) motion for attorney fees is denied.

Plaintiff contracted to finance Galca Group LLC based on Galca's accounts receivables. That contract provided for assignment to plaintiff of security interests in the receivables and had a prevailing-party attorney fee provision. (Civ. Code 1717.) In turn, Galca had an oral contract for temporary staffing services with TPI (SAC at 4:27-5:3), generating receivables assigned to plaintiff. Galca was suspended as an LLC in 2017. (Memo. 3:24-4:3.) Plaintiff sued, alleging TPI did not pay receivables as required, but TPI prevailed based on Galca's suspension.

TPI concedes that it "was not a party" to the plaintiff-Galca contract and no one claims the Galca-TPI contract had an attorney fee provision. (Memo. 7:21.) Despite the commonness of accounts receivable financing, TPI's memorandum cites no case that has ever awarded attorney fees in such a scenario. Santisas v. Goodin (1998) 17 Cal.4th 599 regards sale of a residence. (See Memo. 8:26.)

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (302/RBU)

Notice Of Motion And Motion Of Defendant And Cross-Claimant Tijuana Produce, Inc. Seeking To Change Venue; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities; Declarations Of Hector Castrellon, Argelia Castrellon, Jose M. Sandoval, Fanny Hernandez And Rosendo Gonzalez In Support Thereof
Off Calendar Sep-18-2019 Denied
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Sonia Shah, Esq. 408-286-5100 of Pahl & McCay for Plaintiff through Court Call.
Rosendo Gonzalez, Esq. 213-452-0070 of Gonzalea & Associates for Defendant TIJUANA'S PRODUCE, INC. through Court Call.

RULING - A R G U E D. The Court announces the email contesting the tentative ruling was not received timely. Defendant argues third party declaration. Plaintiff request to uphold the tentative ruling. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT/CROSS-CLAIMANT TIJUANA'S PRODUCE, INC.'s Motion Seeking To Change Venue.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. Defendant Tijuana's Produce, Inc.'s motion to change venue under Code Civ. Proc. 397(c) is denied. Section 397(c) is discretionary, and permits transfer from one proper county to another proper county when "the convenience of witnesses and the ends of justice would be promoted by the change." The burden is on the party seeking a change of venue to prove that both the convenience of witnesses and the ends of justice would be promoted by the change. (Corfee v. Southern California Edison Co. (1962) 202 Cal.App.2d 473, 477.) Here, defendant fails to show that the convenience of witnesses would be promoted by a change in venue from San Francisco County to Los Angeles County.

Under a 397(c) motion, "only convenience to third-party disinterested witnesses" may be considered. (Corfee, 202 Cal.App.2d at 478.) The convenience of the parties or their agents or employees may not be considered. (See also Stute v. Burinda (1981) 123 Cal.App.Supp.3d 11, 17 ["Convenience to witnesses only is important, and not the convenience of parties or employees or employees of parties"].) Thus, defendant's primary reliance on the purported inconvenience of the San Francisco forum to its own employees is misplaced. Defendant identifies only one third-party witness: Jose Guillermo Alcantar of Two Star Personnel Services ("TSPS"). The rule against party-witnesses does not apply when an employee is being called to testify by an opposing party. (J.C. Millett Co. v. Latchford -Marble Glass Co. (1959) 167 Cal.App.2d 218, 227.) TSPS has been named as a cross-defendant.

A showing that the convenience of witnesses will be promoted by a change of witness must be done "through affidavits which contain something more than generalities and conclusions." (Corfee, 202 Cal.App.2d at 477.) Rather, "the supporting declarations must show (1) the name of each witness, (2) the expected testimony of each witness, and (3) facts showing why the attendance of said witnesses at trial will be inconvenient at trial and why the ends of justice would be served by a transfer." (Stute, 123 Cal.App.3d Supp. at 17.) Here, defendant has not submitted Mr. Alcantar's declaration, nor has it made any factual showing why it would be inconvenient for him to attend trial, other than the bare fact that TSPS is located in Downey in Southern California. As to defendant's prediction that unnamed representatives of other companies that defendant believes were subject to similar staffing agreements with TSPS will testify, defendant's showing does not satisfy any of the required elements, and in any event, the admissibility of such testimony in this action is doubtful at best.

Defendant's motion to change venue under the doctrine of forum non conveniens is likewise denied, as inapplicable to this matter. The doctrine applies when "in the interest of substantial justice an action should be heard in a forum outside this state." (Archibald v. Cinerama Hotels (1976) 15 Cal. 3d 853, 857.)(as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

RULING - A R G U E D. Defendant argues whether notice is effective. Plaintiff responds. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT TIJUANA'S PRODUCE, INC.'S MOTION SEEKING TO CHANGE VENUE.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling.

Defendant Tijuana's Produce, Inc.'s motion to change venue is denied. On a motion to change venue, the burden is on the moving party to show (1) that venue is proper in a different county than the one in which the lawsuit was filed and (2) that venue is improper in the current county under any applicable theory. (La Mirada Community Hospital v. Superior Court (1967) 249 Cal.App.2d 39, 42.) Although Defendant has established that Los Angeles County would be a proper venue, it has failed to show that venue is improper in San Francisco County under any applicable theory.
